2
Processing large-scale graphs, containing billions of entities, is
0
critical across fields like bioinformatics, high-performance comput-
2
   ing, navigation and route planning, among others. Efficient graph
t
c partitioning, which divides a graph into sub-graphs while mini-
mizing inter-block edges, is essential to graph processing, as it
O
  optimizes parallel computing and enhances data locality. Tradi-
0  tional in-memory partitioners, such as METIS and KaHIP, offer
1
  high-quality partitions but are often infeasible for enormous graphs
 
] due to their substantial memory overhead. Streaming partitioners
reduce memory usage to O(𝑛), where 𝑛 is the number of nodes
S
of the graph, by loading nodes sequentially and assigning them
D
.  to blocks on-the-fly. This paper introduces StreamCpi, a novel
s
framework that further reduces the memory overhead of streaming
c
[ partitioners through run-length compression of block assignments.
 
  Notably, StreamCpi enables the partitioning of trillion-edge graphs
1 on edge devices. Additionally, within this framework, we propose
v a modification to the la_vector bit vector for append support,
2 which can be used for online run-length compression in other
3
streaming applications. Empirical results show that StreamCpi
7
reduces memory usage while maintaining or improving partition
7
quality. For instance, using StreamCpi, the Fennel partitioner ef-
0
.  fectively partitions a graph with 17 billion nodes and 1.03 trillion
0 edges on a Raspberry Pi, achieving significantly better solution
1
quality than Hashing, the only other feasible algorithm on edge
4
devices. StreamCpi thus advances graph processing by enabling
2
: high-quality partitioning on low-cost machines.
v
i
Source Code Availability:
X
r The open-source software has been made available at https://github.com/
a
KaHIP/CompressedStreamingGraphPartitioning and https://github.com/
kurpicz/cpi.

Large-scale graphs, often consisting of billions of entities, are em-
ployed across diverse disciplines to model social, biological, naviga-
tional, and technical networks. Efficiently processing these graphs
can uncover valuable insights – such as community structures in
social networks or pathways in biological systems – determine
navigational and recommendation systems and run scientific simu-
lations, among other applications [2, 3, 17, 22, 30, 34, 48]. However,
processing huge graphs requires extensive computational resources,
and concurrently, there is an ever-growing need for scalable graph
processing techniques. Several graph-based applications are either

Graphs on Edge Devices
Kurpicz  Christian Schulz
te of Technology  University of Heidelberg
kit.edu  christian.schulz@informatik.uni-
heidelberg.de
Daniel Seemaier
Karlsruhe Institute of Technology
daniel.seemaier@kit.edu
preceded by or modeled as graph partitioning. In graph partition-
ing, large graphs are divided into sub-graphs distributed among
𝑘 blocks of roughly equal size while minimizing the number of
inter-block edges. Graph partitioning is crucial for optimizing load
balancing in parallel computing, enhancing the performance of
search algorithms, and improving data locality in large-scale graph
processing. However, graph partitioning is NP-hard [11, 33] and
there can be no approximation algorithm with a constant ratio fac-
tor for general graphs unless P = NP [14]. Thus, heuristic algorithms
are used in practice.
Traditional graph partitioners, like METIS [44] and KaHIP [59],
load the complete graph in memory before partitioning it. These
in-memory partitioners produce high solution quality, as they use
complete global information to compute partitions, but have a high
memory overhead, as they store the entire node set in memory.
There is an increasing need to partition huge graphs with less com-
putational resources, especially in scenarios where real-time pro-
cessing is critical, such as online social network monitoring, fraud
detection in financial networks, and dynamic routing in transporta-
tion systems. Further, reducing the memory requirements of graph
partitioning can lead to significant reductions in monetary costs,
as large graphs can be partitioned on small, relatively inexpensive
machines. Responding to the need for partitioners with low mem-
ory overhead, streaming partitioners load nodes sequentially for
immediate assignment to blocks, in lieu of loading the entire node
set, thus consuming less memory than in-memory partitioners.
While streaming partitioners typically yield worse solution qual-
ity than in-memory approaches, as they lack complete global knowl-
edge, recently there is a push to enhance their performance by
developing more sophisticated streaming techniques that incorpo-
rate partial global information [1, 4, 20, 26–28, 39, 43, 51, 63, 64].
These partitioners maintain information about blocks assigned to
nodes streamed previously to inform future assignments, and sub-
sequently compute a score or objective function for every node
for each block 𝑘, based on information from previous assignments,
and then assign that node to the block with the best score. While
streaming partitioning using partial global knowledge increases
solution quality, it leads to a higher memory overhead, particularly
for large graphs, as block assignments are stored for all nodes, typ-
ically in a container of size of Θ(𝑛). Thus, there remains scope to
significantly lower the memory overhead for high-quality stream-
ing graph partitioning, by reducing the memory requirements of
storing block assignments.

no multiple or self-edges, such that 𝑛 = |𝑉 |, 𝑚 = |𝐸|. Let 𝑐 : 𝑉 →

weight function. We generalize 𝑐 and 𝜔 functions to sets, such that

problem pertains to the partitioning of a graph into 𝑘 smaller
graphs by assigning the nodes of the graph to 𝑘 mutually exclu-
sive blocks, such that the blocks have roughly the same size and
the particular objective function is minimized. More precisely, a

𝑘
Streaming Partitioning Model. Streaming algorithms typi-
cally follow a load-compute-store logic. The most commonly used
streaming model is the one-pass model where nodes are loaded
sequentially and are permanently assigned to blocks. For each node,
a score is computed for all blocks of the partition. The scoring
function indicates how well a node is connected to a block, while
maintaining balance constraints, by examining blocks assignments
of previously streamed neighbors of the current node. The node is
then permanently assigned to the block with the highest score.
2

Run Length Compression. Given a sequence 𝑆 of length 𝑛, a
run is a substring of the sequence 𝑆 [𝑖 .. 𝑗 ] where 1 ≤ 𝑖 ≤ 𝑗 ≤ 𝑛, with
𝑖 being the starting index and 𝑗 being the ending index of the run,
satisfying the following conditions:
(1) 𝑆 [ℓ] = 𝑆 [ℓ + 1] for all 𝑖 ≤ ℓ < 𝑗 .
(2)  If 𝑖 > 1, then 𝑆 [𝑖 − 1] ≠ 𝑆 [𝑖].
(3)  If 𝑗 < 𝑛, then 𝑆 [ 𝑗 ] ≠ 𝑆 [ 𝑗 + 1].
In other words, a run is a maximal consecutive sequence of identical
𝑆 1  𝑆 [𝑖 .. 𝑗 ]  𝑗 −𝑖 +
characters in  .  The run length of a run  is defined as  1.
The run head of a run 𝑆 [𝑖 .. 𝑗 ] is defined as the character 𝑆 [𝑖] (which
repeats itself throughout the run).
Then, the run-length compressed sequence RLC(𝑆) is a sequence
of tuples consisting of each run’s length and its corresponding run
head, for example, 𝑆 = aaabaabbba is represented as RLC(𝑆) =
⟨a, 3⟩, ⟨b, 1⟩, ⟨a, 2⟩, ⟨b, 3⟩, ⟨a, 1⟩.
Rank and Select Support. The foundation for many compact and
compressed data structures is the bit vector, a sequence over the
binary alphabet {0, 1} with rank and select support [36, 37, 53].
For a bit vector 𝐵 of length 𝑛 and 𝛼 ∈ {0, 1}, the rank and select
operations are defined as follows:
• rank𝛼 (𝑖) = |{ 𝑗 ≤ 𝑖 : 𝐵[ 𝑗 ] = 𝛼}| for 𝑖 ∈ [0, 𝑛), which counts
the number of occurrences of the bit 𝛼 in the bit vector up
to and including the 𝑖-th position.
• select𝛼 (𝑥) = min{ 𝑗 : rank𝛼 ( 𝑗 ) = 𝑥}, where 𝑥 ≤ rank𝛼 (𝑛 −
1), which finds the position of the 𝑥-th occurrence of the
bit 𝛼 in the bit vector.
These operations can be generalized to alphabets of arbitrary size,
extending their utility to various text mining, data compression,
and indexing problems.
3  RELATEDWORK
We refer the reader to recent surveys on graph partitioning for
relevant literature [15, 18, 61]. Here, we focus on the research on
streaming graph partitioning.
Streaming Graph Partitioning. Tsourakakis et al. [64] introduce
Fennel, a one-pass partitioning heuristic adapted from the clus-
tering objective modularity [12]. In this work, we use a modified
Fennel scoring function for partitioning on-the-fly. A detailed de-
scription of the Fennel objective function is provided in Section 4.
LDG, a greedy heuristic proposed by Stanton and Kliot [62], is an-
other streaming partitioning scoring function. Both Fennel and
LDG require knowledge of the blocks assigned to the neighbors
of the currently streamed node, and the weight of the 𝑘 partition
blocks, i.e., the sum of the weight of nodes assigned to the blocks.
As a consequence, these partitioners require O(𝑛 + 𝑘) memory to
store an array of block assignments of size 𝑛 and the weights of all
blocks.

ReLDG and ReFennel are re-streaming versions of LDG
and Fennel [54], which also require O(𝑛 + 𝑘) memory to store
block assignments and block weights. In practice, 𝑛 >> 𝑘.
An alternative to one-pass streaming is the buffered streaming
model, in which a small portion of the graph is stored in-memory.
Buffered partitioners like HeiStream [27], which performs multi-
level partitioning using a weighted version of the Fennel scoring
1In this paper, our simplified run definition, i.e., with fixed period 𝑝 =  , is sufficient.
1
The period is a positive integer 𝑝 such that 𝑆 [𝑖 ] = 𝑆 [𝑖 + 𝑝 ], e.g., ababab is a run with
period 2..

Contribution. In this work, we propose a framework, Stream-
Cpi, that uses run-length compression of block assignments to im-
prove the memory efficiency of streaming graph partitioners. With
this framework, we introduce a highly memory-efficient stream-
ing graph partitioner capable of partitioning trillion edge graphs
with billions of nodes on edge devices. StreamCpi applies problem-
tailored run-length data compression techniques, and modified
scoring functions to mitigate the memory bottleneck that stor-
ing block assignments causes. To enable data compression within
this framework, we provide a modification to the state-of-the-art
la_vector learned bit vector to enable append support. This mod-
ification supports on-the-fly run-length compression, and is thus
suitable for streaming algorithms across various applications.
By reducing memory overhead significantly while maintaining
high-quality partitioning, our framework increases the feasibility
of graph partitioning, by enabling high-quality partitioning of huge
graphs on low-cost machines. For example, our partitioner success-
fully partitions a generated graph with 17 billion nodes and 1.03
trillion edges on a Raspberry Pi with 100% better solution quality
than Hashing, the only other algorithm that can partition this
graph on a Raspberry Pi.

metadata.section_title: StreamCpi: A Framework for Memory Optimization in Streaming Partitioning

text:
OPTIMIZATION IN STREAMING
PARTITIONING
In this section, we introduce our framework StreamCpi for re-
ducing the memory consumption of streaming partitioners by
Compressing the array of block assignments (Partition Indices)
used by such partitioners. In particular, we apply run-length data
compression to encode runs of repeating block assignments gen-
erated by the streaming partitioner on-the-fly. We thus propose a
novel (semi-)dynamic compression vector that functions as a drop-
in replacement to standard arrays, like std::vector in C++, used to
store block assignments in streaming graph partitioners. In this
work, we build StreamCpi with a modified Fennel partitioning
scoring function, the details of which are provided below. Princi-
pally, our framework can be inserted as a subroutine to reduce the
memory footprint in any streaming graph partitioning tool that
needs to store block IDs in a vector of size Θ(𝑛).
Fennel [64] assigns a node 𝑣 to the block 𝑉𝑖  that maximizes
the Fennel gain function |𝑉𝑖 ∩ 𝑁 (𝑣) | − 𝑓 ( |𝑉𝑖 |), where 𝑓 ( |𝑉𝑖 |) is
a penalty function to respect a balancing threshold. The authors
𝛾−1
define the balancing penalty 𝑓 ( |𝑉𝑖 |) = 𝛼𝛾 · |𝑉𝑖 |  , in which 𝛾 is
𝑘𝛾−1
a free parameter and 𝛼 = 𝑚  𝛾  . After parameter tuning, the au-
𝑛
thors set 𝛾 = 1.5. The pseudocode of the algorithm is given in
Algorithm 1. As described in Section 3, the memory efficiency of
Fennel’s scoring function, like that of other streaming partitioning
scoring functions, is limited by the need to store an array of block
assignments 𝐴𝑝𝑎𝑟𝑡 . This array, 𝐴𝑝𝑎𝑟𝑡 , has size Θ(𝑛), and stores the
block assigned to each node in its corresponding index. In Fennel,
computing |𝑉𝑖 ∩ 𝑁 (𝑣) | requires knowing which blocks 𝐴𝑝𝑎𝑟𝑡 [𝑤]
were assigned to the neighbors 𝑤 of node 𝑣. Any neighbors that
have not been streamed yet are ignored. Additionally, computing
𝛾−1
the penalty 𝑓 ( |𝑉𝑖 |) = 𝛼𝛾 · |𝑉𝑖 |  requires knowing the size of each
block 𝑉𝑖 . These are also stored in an array of size Θ(𝑘).
The overall memory complexity of Fennel is thus O(𝑛 + 𝑘), to
store block assignments and block sizes respectively. Typically, the
number of nodes 𝑛 of the graph is much larger than the number of
blocks 𝑘 in the partition, and thus, the array of block assignments
𝐴𝑝𝑎𝑟𝑡 dominates memory consumption. In StreamCpi, we replace
𝐴𝑝𝑎𝑟𝑡 with a run-length compressed vector to save space. More
specifically, we compress runs of block assignments by storing the
head of the run and its length as defined in Section 2. For example,
1,1,1,1,2,2,3,3,4,5 is stored as ⟨1, 4⟩, ⟨2, 2⟩, ⟨3, 2⟩, ⟨4, 1⟩, ⟨5, 1⟩.
A schematic of using naive run-length compression with streaming
partitioning is shown in Figure 1.
To support streaming with access to block assignments, the run-
length compressed vector must support append (for storing block
assignments of nodes as they are streamed) and query (for score
computation). Thus, we propose a (semi-)dynamic compression
vector that can perform run-length compression on-the-fly using a
bit vector that can perform queries in constant time, and supports
append. Next, we provide the details of this data structure in Section
4.1. To improve this approach further, we present a batch-wise

all nodes’ block assignments. Block assignments are used to co
with the higher score. Run-length encoding of block assignmen
Algorithm 1 Fennel Partitioning Algorithm
1:  Input: Graph 𝐺 = (𝑉 , 𝐸), number of blocks 𝑘
2: Output: Partition of nodes into 𝑘 blocks

compression model to speed up append and query times in Section
4.2, and offer a simple modification of the Fennel scoring function
in Section 4.3 for greater memory reduction by encouraging longer
runs of block assignments. Finally, in Section 4.4, we discuss an
alternative approach for the memory optimization of streaming
partitioners by using an external-memory priority queue with time-
forward processing to store and access block assignments.
4.1  Run-Length Compression of Block
Assignments
We propose a (semi-)dynamic run-length compressed vector to store
and access block assignments during streaming partitioning, in
place of a standard array container.

metadata.section_title: StreamCpi: A Framework for Memory Optimization in Streaming Partitioning

text:
In our implementation, we use
a bit vector with rank and select support to perform run-length com-
pression. More specifically, we utilize the la_vector bit vector [7],
modified to support the append function, which offers efficient

random access and rank queries through learning and adapting to
data regularities. We select la_vector over alternatives because
it offers a good trade-off between (rank) query performance and
compression ratio on a diverse set of inputs, as generated by the
streaming partitioner for different graph types and 𝑘 values.
We begin by describing how a bit vector with rank and select sup-
port can be used to perform run-length compression of a sequence.
Let 𝑟 be the number of runs contained in the sequence 𝑆 that we
wish to compress. First, we store the run heads of runs in 𝑆 in an
array 𝐴  of length 𝑟 . To match indices of the elements in 𝑆 to
head
their corresponding run head in𝐴  , we utilize a bit vector 𝐵 with
head
rank support. The bit vector 𝐵 has length 𝑛 and contains a 1-bit at
the same index as the starting index of each run in 𝑆, and a 0-bit oth-
erwise, as shown in Figure 2. The bit vector thus contains 𝑟 1-bits.
To determine the run head of the 𝑣-th element in the uncompressed
sequence 𝑆, we use a single rank query on the bit vector to identify
the index of the run head in 𝐴ℎ𝑒𝑎𝑑 : 𝜌𝑣 = rank1(𝑣) − 1. This process
is depicted in Figure 2 for 𝑣 = 6. Note that each start of a run is
marked by a 1-bit in 𝐵. Therefore, the number of 1-bits up to and
including position 𝑣 corresponds to the run in which position 𝑣
resides. Finally, we return the corresponding run-head 𝐴  [𝜌𝑣].
head
Storing the bit vector 𝐵 plainly in memory requires 𝑛 bits: to
save more space, the bit vector is compressed as well. In this work,
we use the la_vector compressed bit vector for more efficient
query and space saving. In la_vector, the compressed bit vector is
represented by piece-wise linear approximations and some correc-
tion terms. More specifically, instead of representing the bit vector
directly, la_vector maps each element of the uncompressed se-
quence to a point in the Cartesian plane. Then, it attempts to build a
function that fits these points and can thus be queried to recover the
original element. However, finding a function that fits all points can
be challenging, hard to compute, or occupy too much space. Thus,

B  1  0  0  1  0  1  0  1  0  1
bit vector
n
ρ6 = rank1(6) − 1
= 3 − 1 = 2
Figure 2: Run-length compression of a sequence 𝑆 using a
bit vector 𝐵 and an array of run heads 𝐴head. The sequence
and bit vector have size 𝑛. The bit vector contains 1-bit if a
new run starts at the corresponding position in 𝑆, or 0-bit
otherwise. In practice, the bit vector is itself compressed.
Computing 𝑟𝑎𝑛𝑘1(𝑖) of an index 𝑖 returns the number of 1’s
in the bit vector that occur before index 𝑖 in 𝐵. Here, 𝑖 = 6
and 𝜌6 = 𝑟𝑎𝑛𝑘1(6) − 1 returns the position of the run in 𝐴head
that 𝑖 = 6 belongs to, i.e, 𝐴head[𝜌6] = 𝐴head[2] = 3 = 𝑆 [6].
points of the plane are split into segments, 𝑠1, . . . , 𝑠ℓ , that are each
represented by their own linear functions 𝑓𝑗  for 𝑗 ∈ [1, ℓ] using
Piecewise Linear Approximation. For each segment, only the linear
function and some correction terms are stored, making la_vector
very space efficient. Further, la_vector supports rank queries in
time proportional to the log of the number of segments constructed
to represent the sequence, which is very fast in practice [7].
However, la_vector only considers the static compression case,
that is, it constructs a compressed bit vector for a sequence that is al-
ready built and can not be modified. However, while streaming, we
build the sequence of block assignments on-the-fly, and therefore
require the ability to append to the bit vector. Therefore, as an addi-
tional contribution of independent interest, we modify la_vector
to be (semi-)dynamic by adding support for an append operation.
To incorporate append support in la_vector, we periodically
decompress the last segment of the compressed la_vector repre-
sentation built so far, append new elements to it, and then compress
it again. However, doing this for every append operation would
be very time consuming. Thus, we introduce a buffer of size 𝛿 that
stores the starting positions of the most recent 𝛿 uncompressed
runs.

metadata.section_title: StreamCpi: A Framework for Memory Optimization in Streaming Partitioning

text:
Once the buffer is full, the last segment of the la_vector rep-
resentation is decompressed, and all starting positions are appended
to the representation in a batch. This allows our (semi-)dynamic
compressed bit vector to have size, i.e., memory consumption, close
to the static version, while reducing the time taken to append to it.
However, our (semi-)dynamic compressed bit vector still has
some shortcomings. Firstly, compared to using a standard array,
append and query times are still longer. Secondly, in the case of
streaming partitioning, when compressing block assignments on-
the-fly, the efficiency of compression largely depends on the order
in which nodes are streamed, the locality inherent in the graph
being partitioned, and consequently, if the block assignments pro-
duced by the partitioner exhibit long or short runs. Thus, an efficient
run-length compressed data structure can significantly reduce mem-
ory consumption if  the generated partition data contains many
repeated block assignments for consecutive nodes. We propose
two modes for StreamCpi to address these shortcomings in the
upcoming subsections, namely batch-wise compression to improve
5

runtime, and a Fennel modification to encourage block assign-
ments with longer runs.
4.2  Batch-Wise Compression
Streaming partitioning scoring functions, like Fennel, require fre-
quent access to block assignments of previously streamed nodes.
For every node 𝑣, we have up to |𝑁 (𝑣) | such queries. While standard
array containers have random access times of O(1), our compres-
sion vector requires query time proportional to the number of
segments in its bit vector representation as detailed in Section 4.1.
The number of segments in this representation is likely to be higher
for an input of larger size. Thus, to speed up query times from
our compression vector, we split the input to be compressed in
batches. In particular, instead of using one compression vector re-
sponsible for compressing block assignments for all 𝑛 nodes, we
create 𝑛/𝛽 smaller compression vectors, where 𝛽 is an input param-
eter specifying the number of nodes whose block assignments we
wish to store in a single compression vector. We then insert and
access block assignments as follows.
Let the nodes of the graph be given IDs 𝑣 ∈ {0, 1, ..., (𝑛 − 1)}. We
append the block assignment of node 𝑣 to the 𝑗 -th compression
vector where 𝑗 = 𝑣/𝛽 (positive integer division, rounded down).
Subsequently, since we stream and append blocks assigned to nodes
in monotonically increasing order of node IDs, we can retrieve
the block assigned to 𝑣 from the 𝑣%𝛽 position of the 𝑗 = 𝑣/𝛽-th
compression vector. This way, we query block assignments from
compression vectors with a fewer number of segments, thereby
getting faster access times. We demonstrate the speed up offered
by this approach in Section 5.1.1.
4.3  Fennel Modification
As mentioned previously, the efficiency of on-the-fly compression
of block assignments is greatly affected by whether the partitioner’s
block assignments create long or short runs. We thus motivate our
partitioner to generate long runs by incentivizing it to place the
currently streamed node into the block assigned to the previously
streamed node. To do so, we modify the score generated by the
Fennel objective function by introducing a scaling parameter 𝜅
as follows:
Let 𝑏prev be the block assigned to the previously streamed node.
Then, after computing the Fennel score 𝑆 for block𝑏 for the current
node, get an updated score 𝑆new by calling
(cid:40)  (cid:16)  sgn(𝑆 ) (cid:17)
𝑆 ·  𝜅  if 𝑏 = 𝑏prev
𝑆new = 𝑓 (𝑏, 𝑆) =
𝑆  if 𝑏 ≠ 𝑏prev
The function 𝑓 (𝑏, 𝑆) takes two parameters: 𝑏, which refers to
the partition block, and 𝑆, which refers to the score produced by
the Fennel objective function for block 𝑏. If block 𝑏 is the same as
sgn(𝑥 )
the previous block 𝑏prev, then the score 𝑆 is multiplied by 𝜅  .
Here, sgn(𝑥) returns 1 for positive 𝑥, −1 for negative 𝑥, and 0 for
𝑥 = 0. So, the function effectively multiplies positive scores by 𝜅
and divides negative scores by 𝜅. If block 𝑏 is different from the
previous block 𝑏prev, then the score 𝑆 remains unchanged.
The 𝜅 parameter ensures that the score returned by the Fen-
nel objective function is scaled by a factor of 𝜅 for the previously
assigned block, even if the score produced a negative value. As a.

Setup. We implemented the (semi-)dynamic run-length compres-
sion vector as a standalone templated container in C++. We use this
compression vector in StreamCpi, along with a modified Fennel
scoring function which drops runtime dependency on 𝑘 [26]. Any
scoring function, like LDG could be used in place of Fennel, and
StreamCpi can be plugged into any buffered streaming partitioner.
StreamCpi is compiled using gcc 13.2.0 with full optimization en-
abled (-O3 flag). All parameter study experiments were performed
on a single core of a machine with a sixteen-core Intel Xeon Silver
4216 processor running at 2.1 GHz, 100 GB of main memory, 16
MB of L2-Cache, and 22 MB of L3-Cache running Ubuntu 20.04.1.
To demonstrate the extent of the capabilities of our framework,
we additionally perform experiments on a Raspberry Pi 5 with a
Broadcom BCM2712 2.4 GHz quad-core 64-bit Arm Cortex-A76
CPU and 8GB of LPDDR4X-4267 SDRAM.
Instances. Our graph instances for experiments are shown
in Table 1 and are sourced from Ref. [5, 8–10, 31, 49, 57]. All
instances evaluated have been used for benchmarking in previ-
ous works on graph partitioning. We set the number of blocks to
6

𝑘  { 1,  2, . . . ,  8}
= 2  2  2  for tuning and test experiments. We allow an
imbalance of 𝜀 = 3% for all partitioners. While streaming, we use
the default order of nodes in these graphs, i.e., we do not change
the ordering of nodes in the sourced instances.
Streaming Graph Generation. To evaluate our framework on
large-scale graphs, we developed a streaming version of the Ka-
Gen graph generator [31, 60], which supports a variety of graph
generation models, including random geometric, random Delaunay,
random hyperbolic, and Barabási-Albert graphs. This streaming
generator mitigates the I/O constraints typical of resource-limited
platforms like the Raspberry Pi and will be made available as open-
source software for users to experiment with streaming graph al-
gorithms across different applications.
Our streaming approach allows flexible selection of both the
graph model and the construction parameters, such as the number
of nodes or edges. The generator sequentially returns either an
edge list or the neighborhood of each successive node, adhering to
common graph storage formats. Internally, the graph is constructed
incrementally in chunks, meaning only a small portion of the graph
resides in memory at any given time. Here, on-the-fly graph gener-
ation is made possible using a communication-free paradigm that
involves re-computation with psuedorandom hashing and various
divide-and-conquer schemes, as detailed in [31, 60]. The number
of chunks is controlled by the user, providing a trade-off between
runtime and memory consumption.
In this work, we conduct experiments on randomly generated
billion-node, trillion-edge graphs using the Barabási-Albert (BA)
and random geometric (RGG) models to represent large scale-free
networks and mesh-like graphs, respectively. We set the average
−5
degree to 60 for the BA graphs and use a radius of 4.71536 × 10
for the RGG graphs. These parameters, which are within the typical
ranges observed in real-world networks, allowus to generate graphs
with a comparable number of edges across both models.
Methodology. We measure running time, edge cut, and memory
consumption, i.e., the maximum resident set size for the executed
process. When averaging over all instances, we use the geometric
mean to give every instance the same influence on the final score.
Let the runtime, edge cut, or memory consumption be denoted
by the score 𝜎𝐴 for some 𝑘-partition generated by an algorithm
𝐴. We express this score relative to others using the following
tools: improvement over an algorithm 𝐵, computed as a percentage
𝜎𝐴
( 𝜎  − 1) ∗ 100% and relative value over an algorithm 𝐵, computed
𝐵
𝜎𝐴
as 𝜎𝐵 . Additionally, we present performance profiles by Dolan and
Moré [25] to benchmark our algorithms. These profiles relate the
running time (resp. solution quality, memory) of the slower (resp.
worse) algorithms to the fastest (resp. best) one on a per-instance
basis, rather than grouped by 𝑘. Their 𝑥-axis shows a factor 𝜏 while
their 𝑦-axis shows the fraction of instances for which an algorithm
has up to 𝜏 times the running time (resp. solution quality, memory)
of the fastest (resp. best) algorithm.
5.1  Parameter Study
We begin by exploring possible variations to the parameters used
in StreamCpi, with different memory-runtime trade-offs, as de-
scribed in Section 4, namely the use of batch-wise compression
to increase append speed and a modified Fennel scoring function

in almost all instances.
5.1.2  Fennel Modification. Modifying the Fennel scoring func-
tion with a 𝜅 scaling parameter, as described in Section 4.3, re-
duces both memory and runtime overhead, with our experiments
indicating that 𝜅 = 20 performs best. Experiments on the Tun-
ing Set demonstrate that, on average, StreamCpi𝛽 𝑛,𝜅  is 5.4×
=  =5
faster, and uses 1.6× less memory than StreamCpi𝛽 𝑛,𝜅  . With
=  =1
StreamCpi𝛽 𝑛,𝜅  , the partitioner is 6.3× faster and uses 1.7×
=  =20
less memory on average, compared to StreamCpi𝛽=𝑛,𝜅=1. We de-
termine 𝜅 = 20 to be a suitable 𝜅-value for memory and runtime
efficiency, as further increases to 𝜅 yield comparable or worse
memory, runtime and solution quality trade-offs. Figure 4 demon-
strates that using 𝜅-modification produces lower memory over-
head and faster runtime compared to StreamCpi𝛽=𝑛,𝜅=1 in all
instances. However, our results show that 𝜅-modified Stream-
Cpi in Tuning Set instances produces worse solution quality than
StreamCpi𝛽=𝑛,𝜅=1; on average across all instances, StreamCpi
with 𝜅 = 1 produces between 7.7% to 10% better solution quality
than StreamCpi with 𝜅 = 5 up to 𝜅 = 20. Note, however, as Figure 4
8

demonstrates, 𝜅-modified StreamCpi produces better solution qual-
ity than StreamCpi𝛽=𝑛,𝜅=1 in approximately 10% of instances. In
these instances, nodes are streamed based on some notion of high
locality, i.e., nodes are ordered close to their neighbors.
In summary, our parameter study offers two categories of modes
for StreamCpi: the batch-wise compression mode to optimize run-
time, at the cost of memory overhead, and the 𝜅-modified Stream-
Cpi to optimize both runtime and memory, at the potential cost
of solution quality.
5.2  Comparison with Standard Array, External
Memory Priority Queue and Hashing
We present experimental results from the Test Set depicting the
relative performance of StreamCpi using the different modes of
our compression vector – default StreamCpi𝛽=𝑛,𝜅=1, batch-wise
StreamCpi𝛽  𝑘,𝜅  , and𝜅-modified StreamCpi𝛽 𝑛,𝜅  – as com-
=100  =1  =  =20
pared to Fennel with a standard array (hereafter ‘Fennel-Array’),
and using the external memory priority queue (hereafter ‘Fennel-
ExtPQ’) that we presented in Section 4.4. Here, we exclude re-
sults for StreamCpi using 𝜅-modification in combination with
batch-wise compression, as the overall compression achieved by
setting 𝜅 = 20 is high enough that batch-wise compression does
not offer additional benefits. Figure 5 gives performance profiles
for the Test Set. We find that the use of the 𝜅 modification offers
the best memory optimization, without significantly increasing
runtime: our results show that, on average, StreamCpi𝛽=𝑛,𝜅=20
consumes 9.7× less memory than Fennel-Array, while being 1.5×
slower. StreamCpi𝛽=𝑛,𝜅=20 uses the least memory in almost all
Test instances, with a comparable runtime to Fennel-Array (Fig-
ure 5). Surprisingly, due to high locality in the graph instances,
StreamCpi𝛽=𝑛,𝜅=20 produces 76.44% better solution quality on av-
erage than the rest, which all have the same solution quality.
Our experimental results show that, on average, our proposed
StreamCpi framework with tunable compression modes and ex-
ternal memory priority queue all reduce memory overhead, as
compared to Fennel-Array. On average, StreamCpi𝛽=𝑛,𝜅=1 uses
2.2× less memory than Fennel-Array, at the cost of being 8.2×
slower.  The  batch-wise  StreamCpi𝛽=100𝑘,𝜅=1  improves  on
StreamCpi𝛽 𝑛,𝜅  in  runtime,  being  6.9×  slower  than
=  =1
Fennel-Array on average, but uses more memory (2.2× more
memory on average than Fennel-Array). As shown in Figure 5,
both StreamCpi𝛽=𝑛,𝜅=1 and StreamCpi𝛽=100𝑘,𝜅=1 consume less
memory than Fennel-Array for over 80% of all instances, but
have a longer runtime in all instances. Those instances in which
Fennel-Array uses less memory than StreamCpi represent graph
partitions with many short runs of consecutive block assignments,
for which compression is less effective. The use of an external mem-
ory priority queue produces significant memory improvements,
using 5.8× less memory than Fennel-Array on average, but has a
higher runtime cost, as it is on average 12.0× slower than Fennel-
Array. Additionally, it uses less memory than StreamCpi𝛽=𝑛,𝜅=1
in all instances, but is slower than it in about 90% of instances (Fig-
ure 5). This is because the stxxl external memory priority queue
has been configured to use as little data in the internal memory as
possible (internal buffer size of 3MB), reducing memory consump-
tion but increasing runtime due tomore frequent I/O fromdisk.

advantage of Fennel-ExtPQ is that its memory consumption is

In summary, all the StreamCpi compression modes and exter-
nal memory priority queue that we propose reduce the memory
consumption of Fennel-Array, i.e., the Fennel partitioner us-
ing a standard array to store block assignments. Employing 𝜅
modification, i.e., StreamCpi𝛽=𝑛,𝜅=20, produces the best compres-
sion, resulting in, on average, memory overhead that is 9.7× less
than Fennel-Array, while being 1.5× slower. Using the 𝜅 mod-
ified StreamCpi𝛽=𝑛,𝜅=20 additionally improves solution quality
by 76.44% on average, as it produces significant solution quality
improvements in high-locality graphs (though it would reduce solu-
tion quality in low-locality graphs). Our default StreamCpi𝛽=𝑛,𝜅=1

5.3  Partitioning on Raspberry Pi
In this section, we provide experiments on a set of massive graphs
shown in Table 1, comparing the performance of 𝜅-modified
StreamCpi𝛽=𝑛,𝜅=20, Fennel-Array and Hashing, run on a Rasp-
berry Pi. Presently, Hashing is the only partitioning method that
can partition several billion-node and sparse trillion edge graphs on
edge devices. Table 2 gives detailed per instance results for instances
of the massive set.
Our experiments on the massive graphs concur with the results
from the Test Set experiments, demonstrating the capabilities of
StreamCpi under extremememory constraints. StreamCpi𝛽=𝑛,𝜅=20
uses lessmemory and produces higher solution quality than Fennel-
Array across all real-world instances with a comparable runtime.
Among real-world networks, the largest improvement in memory
overhead  and  solution  quality  that  we  observed  for.

This paper introduces StreamCpi, a novel framework designed
to significantly reduce the memory overhead of streaming graph
partitioners through run-length compression of block assignments.
StreamCpi is highly memory-efficient and can partition massive
graphs on edge devices.We provide amodification to the la_vector
bit vector to add append support, thus enabling on-the-fly run-
length compression. Compared to Fennel with a standard array,
we demonstrate on Test Set instances that StreamCpi uses, on
average, up to 9.7× less memory with comparable runtime. Our
experiments on a set of massive graphs validate the effectiveness of
StreamCpi, which can successfully partition graphs with a trillion
edges on a Raspberry Pi, demonstrating up to 95.9% improvement
in solution quality over Hashing, the only other method capable of
handling such large graphs on edge devices. These results highlight
the potential of StreamCpi to facilitate high-quality partitioning
of large-scale graphs on low-cost, resource-constrained machines,
significantly advancing the field of graph processing. Further, our
framework is generalizable to other applications of streaming algo-
rithms, which can benefit from online run-length compression.
